response:
## Decision: Adopt Option A — Staged sunset with floor and migration help

**Verdict:** All three advisors converged independently on the same ranking (O1=5, O3=2, O2=1) — no round-2 reveal was even needed since no pair diverged by ≥2. That consensus, on its own priors, from three different cruxes (loud-anecdote veto risk, migration-difficulty asymmetry, indefinite-cost lock-in), is the strongest signal here. I'm going with it.

### Why O1 over the alternatives
- **O2 (indefinite) fails on its own terms.** The memo's stated reason to act is that v1/v2 have diverged enough that every bugfix is written and tested twice. "Until complaints stop" has no convergence condition — complaints are free to make and NorthRoute, Tavo, and Ferrymark have no incentive to ever say "we're done." This locks in the exact cost the memo is trying to escape.
- **O3 (60-day, traffic-only) uses the wrong number.** 0.4% is a share-of-traffic figure, not a cost-of-migration figure. NorthRoute's email describes a parsing-layer rewrite plus a live-dispatcher QA pass — that's not a 60-day lift for a team with "no engineering bandwidth this year" (Tavo). Sizing the runway off the number that isn't the decisive one is the reckless part, not the aggressiveness itself.
- **O1 sizes runway off the actual constraint** (named accounts' switching cost, which the memo admits is unmeasured) while still forcing convergence via a firm date, so three anecdotes can't indefinitely veto a 0.4%-traffic decision.

### What I'm committing to
- **Sunset date:** 2027-01-14 (six months from today, 2026-07-14). Firm — not contingent on complaints stopping.
- **Usage floor:** if v1 traffic is still >0.1% of total geocoding requests (~10k req/day at current volume) at the 5-month mark (2026-12-14), trigger a 60-day hard-cutoff extension review rather than an open-ended slip — extension is a bounded exception, not a reset of the clock.
- **Staged notice schedule:**
  - T-0 (now): direct email to all active v1 callers, led by NorthRoute, Tavo, Ferrymark.
  - T-3 months (2026-10-14): reminder + migration-support offer to any account still sending v1 traffic.
  - T-1 month (2026-12-14): final warning + confirmed cutover date.
  - T-0 (2027-01-14): v1 returns 410 Gone with a link to migration docs.
- **Hands-on migration help**, scoped to the three named accounts (and any other account that requests it before T-3 months):
  - NorthRoute: since their blocker is v1's flat `lat`/`lng`/`label` shape vs. v2's nested `location` object, offer a thin compatibility shim (v2 data reshaped to v1's flat fields) they can point at during their parsing-layer rewrite, so their QA pass isn't blocked on rewriting against v2 directly. This also acts as a fallback for any other undiscovered account with the same coupling.
  - Tavo: given zero bandwidth, this is the account most likely to still be on v1 at T-3 months — flag for direct proactive outreach rather than waiting on them to respond.
  - Ferrymark: same shim path as NorthRoute if their nightly workflow is also shape-coupled; otherwise scope a short paired-migration session.

### Explicit non-goals
No further per-customer migration-cost investigation before committing to this date — the point of O1 is that the fixed deadline plus targeted help substitutes for the missing data rather than waiting on it indefinitely.

options.label: Staged sunset with floor and migration help
options.summary: Set a firm sunset date (e.g. six months out) with a usage floor, staged notices, and hands-on migration assistance for the three loud accounts.

options.label: Indefinite retention
options.summary: Keep v1 running indefinitely, continuing to maintain both code paths until complaints about sunsetting stop.

options.label: Aggressive 60-day sunset
options.summary: Retire v1 within 60 days based solely on the 0.4% traffic share, without further per-customer cost investigation.

O1.crux: Gives the three concrete, named accounts (NorthRoute, Tavo, Ferrymark) real migration runway while still forcing convergence to one code path, without letting three anecdotes indefinitely veto a 0.4%-traffic decision.
O1.dependency: If per-customer migration cost turns out to exceed six months even with hands-on help (e.g. NorthRoute's rewrite plus live-dispatcher QA genuinely can't fit that window), the fixed date could force a rushed, risky cutover for the one customer where it's load-bearing.

O2.crux: Concedes indefinitely to whichever accounts complain loudest, which permanently locks in the exact dual-maintenance cost the memo says is the reason to act, with no mechanism that ever converges.
O2.dependency: If the dual-code-path maintenance burden is actually trivial (e.g. v1 is frozen/no-bugfix-parity truly required), the cost of indefinite retention would be low enough that yielding to the complaints stops being clearly wrong.

O3.crux: Uses the 0.4% traffic-share number as if it were a cost number, when the memo explicitly says the missing input is per-customer migration cost, not traffic share, and three named accounts already show that share and cost are decoupled here.
O3.dependency: If the three complainants are actually outliers and the real per-customer migration cost across the full v1 tail is low (i.e. NorthRoute/Ferrymark's hard-coupling to the flat response shape is rare, not representative), a fast sunset would be far less risky than it looks.

O1.crux: It gives a firm date that ends dual-maintenance while giving the three named, high-difficulty accounts (rewrite-level parsing changes, zero bandwidth) dedicated help and time.
O1.dependency: That the three loud complainants are roughly representative of the hardest remaining v1 migrations — if quieter customers face even costlier migrations, six months plus 'hands-on help for the three' may still leave others stranded.

O2.crux: It never resolves the stated problem (bugfixes written and tested twice) and has no natural end condition, since complaints have no cost to the complainer and thus no reason to stop.
O2.dependency: That dual-maintenance is actually costly today — if v1 were a frozen, low-touch shim rather than a diverging code path needing parallel bugfixes, indefinite retention would be cheap and this score should rise.

O3.crux: It sets the runway using the traffic-share number alone, which is exactly the input the memo says should not decide runway, while ignoring the per-customer migration cost the memo flags as unmeasured and decisive.
O3.dependency: That the three complainants' migrations are actually hard — NorthRoute describes a parsing-layer rewrite plus full QA; if migration cost across affected accounts turns out to be trivial (e.g., a thin adapter), 60 days could be adequate and this score should rise.

O1.crux: gives a firm forward deadline (ends indefinite dual-maintenance) while sizing runway/help to the three named accounts' actual switching cost, which the memo admits is unknown
O1.dependency: if per-customer migration cost turns out trivial (e.g. just a response-shape shim), a shorter fixed date without hands-on help would work just as well and O1's extra assistance cost is wasted

O2.crux: 'until complaints stop' has no natural stopping point and locks in permanent double-maintenance of a diverging codebase for 0.4% of traffic
O2.dependency: if v1/v2 code paths are not actually diverging further (i.e. maintenance burden is flat, not growing), indefinite retention becomes merely wasteful rather than an open-ended and worsening cost

O3.crux: 60 days is set from the aggregate 0.4% traffic number alone, ignoring the memo's own admission that per-customer migration cost — not traffic share — is the number that should set the timeline, and at least one account (NorthRoute) describes a real rewrite, not a config change
O3.dependency: if all three complaining accounts could realistically migrate within 60 days (e.g. the 'rewrite' is smaller than described or bandwidth constraints are temporary), the aggressive timeline stops being reckless and becomes simply decisive

O1.dependency: If per-customer migration cost turns out to exceed six months even with hands-on help (e.g. NorthRoute's rewrite plus live-dispatcher QA genuinely can't fit that window), the fixed date could force a rushed, risky cutover for the one customer where it's load-bearing.
